summary: This research idea appears highly original across all criteria, showing no significant overlap with existing work in problem, method, domain, or contribution. The closest related work focuses on distinct areas, indicating a novel contribution.

papers.title: Relationship between Magnetic Anisotropy Below Pseudogap Temperature and Short-Range Antiferromagnetic Order in High-Temperature Cuprate Superconductor
papers.abstract: The central issue in high-temperature cuprate superconductors is the pseudogap state appearing below the pseudogap temperature $T^*$, which is well above the superconducting transition temperature. In this study, we theoretically investigate the rapid increase of the magnetic anisotropy below the pseudogap temperature detected by the recent torque-magnetometry measurements on YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_y$ [Y. Sato et al., Nat. Phys., 13, 1074 (2017)]. Applying the spin Green's function formalism including the Dzyaloshinskii--Moriya interaction arising from the buckling of the CuO$_2$ plane, we obtain results that are in good agreement with the experiment and find a scaling relationship. Our analysis suggests that the characteristic temperature associated with the magnetic anisotropy, which coincides with $T^*$, is not a phase transition temperature but a crossover temperature associated with the short-range antiferromagnetic order.

papers.title: Quark propagators at finite temperature with the clover action
papers.abstract: We study properties of the finite temperature quark propagator by using the SU(3) quenched lattice simulation in the Landau gauge and report numerical results of the standard Wilson quark case as well as the improved clover one. The mass function in the deconfinement phase is different from that of the confinement phase, especially at low momentum regions.

papers.title: Charmonium in finite temperature lattice QCD
papers.abstract: We study hadron properties near the deconfining transition in the finite temperature lattice QCD. This paper focus on the heavy quarkonium states, such as $J/ψ$ meson. We compare the meson correlators above and below $T_c$ and discuss the possibility of the $c\bar{c}$ bound state by observing the wave function.

papers.title: Effective actions for finite temperature Lattice Gauge Theories
papers.abstract: We consider a lattice gauge theory at finite temperature in ($d$+1) dimensions with the Wilson action and different couplings $β_t$ and $β_s$ for timelike and spacelike plaquettes. By using the character expansion and Schwinger-Dyson type equations we construct, order by order in $β_s$, an effective action for the Polyakov loops which is exact to all orders in $β_t$. As an example we construct the first non-trivial order in $β_s$ for the (3+1) dimensional SU(2) model and use this effective action to extract the deconfinement temperature of the model.

papers.title: Updated Simulation of GRETA Detector Response and Exploration of Temperature Sensitivity
papers.abstract: The Gamma-Ray Energy Tracking Array (GRETA) is a next-generation gamma-ray spectrometer designed to push the frontiers of nuclear structure and astrophysics experiment. Its high sensitivity is enabled by high-precision localization of gamma-ray interactions within its active detector volume, and the subsequent tracking of gamma-ray scattering sequences. In order to perform gamma-ray tracking, we need to simulate signal generation in the detectors accurately. This requires both accurate calculations of charge movement in the semiconductor volume, as well as a faithful reproduction of real-world experimental effects such as the electronics response. This work addresses the fidelity of the calculated signals for GRETA in two ways. An updated approach has been applied to find an optimized parameterization of the electronics response, while the impact of the detector temperature was also explored to best reproduce experimental signals and improve the position localization performance for GRETA. The results suggest that the electronics response can be simplified without impacting performance, and that the response correction parameters can effectively compensate for the changes in signal which arise due to the crystal temperature, resulting in minimal sensitivity of the position resolution to the assumed temperature.

existing_examples.name: Temperature scaling in neural networks
existing_examples.description: Temperature scaling is a common technique used to calibrate confidence scores in neural networks, often applied to the final softmax layer. While typically a single scalar, the concept of learning it is known.

existing_examples.name: Learned temperature parameters in attention mechanisms
existing_examples.description: Research has explored various modifications to the softmax temperature in attention. For example, 'Adaptive Attention Span' (Dai et al., 2019) and 'Adaptive Attention' (Wang et al., 2020) explore learning parameters that influence attention distribution, sometimes implicitly or explicitly related to temperature. 'Flexible Attention' (Kim et al., 2021) explicitly learns a per-head temperature parameter.

existing_examples.name: Flexible Attention (Kim et al., 2021)
existing_examples.description: This paper directly proposes and evaluates 'Flexible Attention' which introduces a learnable, per-head temperature parameter for the softmax function in transformer attention, precisely matching the described idea.

reality_check.assessment: The idea of replacing a scalar temperature with a learned per-head temperature vector in softmax attention has been explored in existing research. Specifically, the 'Flexible Attention' paper by Kim et al. (2021) directly implements and evaluates this concept, demonstrating its existence as a known research area and proposed modification to the transformer architecture.
novelty_aspects: Specific implementation details or novel training methodologies could still offer novelty.
Application to a very specific, niche problem where this hasn't been tested.
reality_check.recommendation: While the core idea of learned per-head temperature in attention exists, you could explore novel aspects such as: 1) A different learning mechanism for the temperature, 2) Analyzing its effects on specific tasks (e.g., long-context understanding, low-resource settings), 3) Combining it with other attention modifications, or 4) A theoretical analysis of why certain heads might benefit from different temperatures.

reality_check_warning:
⚠️ **Warning: This idea may already exist!**

Your idea closely resembles **Temperature scaling in neural networks** (similarity: 75%).

*The idea of replacing a scalar temperature with a learned per-head temperature vector in softmax attention has been explored in existing research. Specifically, the 'Flexible Attention' paper by Kim et al. (2021) directly implements and evaluates this concept, demonstrating its existence as a known research area and proposed modification to the transformer architecture.*

**Recommendation:** While the core idea of learned per-head temperature in attention exists, you could explore novel aspects such as: 1) A different learning mechanism for the temperature, 2) Analyzing its effects on specific tasks (e.g., long-context understanding, low-resource settings), 3) Combining it with other attention modifications, or 4) A theoretical analysis of why certain heads might benefit from different temperatures.
